summary: The post draws a parallel between baby-naming trends from Freakonomics and gendered career patterns, observing that women disproportionately pursue graduate school while men gravitate toward tech and entrepreneurship. Asparouhova notes that graduate enrollment is majority female and declining overall, and recalls that computer programming was once considered women's work until it gained prestige. She hypothesizes that women have never been collectively represented in careers considered powerful by their contemporaries—they arrive only after the status has shifted. She speculates that women will eventually dominate tech founding, but only once it loses its cultural cachet. The post concludes by advising career-minded people to stay ahead of trends rather than follow current prestige signals.
key_points: 58% of first-time graduate students are women, and overall graduate enrollment has been declining—suggesting a gendered divergence in career strategy
The Freakonomics baby-naming pattern (high-status trends trickle down, then lose prestige) may apply to career choices between genders
Programming was once considered women's work; men entered the field as it gained social cachet, displacing women
Women may never have been collectively represented in careers considered powerful at the time they held them
The strategic takeaway is to stay one step ahead of prestige trends rather than following current ones
money_quotes: Does the same thing happen between men and women with their careers?
Maybe graduate school was the thing to do until recently, but now men have turned their eye toward pursuing careers in tech and entrepreneurship, leaving women behind.
Women have simply never been collectively represented in a position considered to be respected and powerful by their contemporaries.
30 years from now, I can confidently predict there will be plenty of women who are tech founders, even if we cease all gender equality efforts right now.
But did that mean we succeeded, if being a tech founder is no longer en vogue?
Rather than focus on getting a graduate degree, learning to code, or starting a company, the goal should be to stay ahead of the trends.
key_insight: Women may consistently arrive in prestigious career fields only after the prestige has migrated elsewhere, suggesting the real strategy is to anticipate where status is heading rather than where it currently resides.
